critic_raw:
The primary model is a deep RL policy network outputting discrete actions (maintain/defer), but the primary evaluation metric is AUPRC. AUPRC requires calibrated probability outputs to rank examples by confidence across the full precision-recall curve. A standard RL policy network trained with discrete action rewards does not naturally output calibrated continuous probabilities — it outputs action preferences (Q-values or policy logits). Computing AUPRC requires converting these to a probability-like score. The design does not describe this conversion, so the RL model may be evaluated on a metric that its output format does not support without an undefined post-processing step.

The model selection criterion on the validation set is stated as 'FNR at precision ≥ 0.70' while the primary evaluation metric is AUPRC. Selecting the best model to minimize FNR at a precision constraint and then reporting AUPRC creates a disconnect: the model optimal for FNR at P≥0.70 is not necessarily the model optimal for AUPRC (which integrates precision-recall tradeoff across all thresholds). The winning model by validation criterion may not be the best model by the reported test metric.

The RL model is trained on historical data with a fixed reward structure, but the reward weights (penalty for false negatives vs. false positives) are listed as hyperparameters tuned on the validation set. In a standard supervised learning framework this is fine, but in RL, reward shaping changes the learned policy's behavior across the entire state-action trajectory, not just the decision boundary. Tuning reward weights on a validation set to optimize a classification metric conflates reward engineering (which changes what the agent optimizes during training) with hyperparameter tuning (which changes how the model trains without altering its objective).

The validation window is only 6 months (months 43–48) while the training window is 30 months. For a sequential RL model that requires sufficient episode rollouts to learn a value function, a 6-month validation window may not cover enough distinct failure episodes to reliably estimate FNR at precision ≥ 0.70 for minority-class events at 8–12% prevalence.
all_issues_raised: The deep RL policy network outputs discrete actions (maintain/defer), not calibrated probabilities, yet AUPRC is the primary metric — AUPRC requires ranked probability scores and the conversion from RL action outputs is undefined.

Model selection optimizes FNR at precision ≥ 0.70 on validation while the primary test metric is AUPRC — the selection criterion and evaluation criterion are misaligned, so the selected model is not guaranteed to optimize the reported metric.

Reward weights are treated as hyperparameters tunable on the validation set, but in RL, reward shaping changes the agent's learned objective during training, not just its decision boundary — this conflates objective specification with hyperparameter tuning.

The validation window is only 6 months for an RL model requiring substantial episode rollouts; at 8–12% failure prevalence, this window may not contain enough failure events for reliable FNR estimation.
all_issues_adjudicated: The deep RL policy network outputs discrete actions (maintain/defer), not calibrated probabilities, yet AUPRC is the primary metric — AUPRC requires ranked probability scores and the conversion from RL action outputs is undefined.

Model selection optimizes FNR at precision ≥ 0.70 on validation while the primary test metric is AUPRC — the selection criterion and evaluation criterion are misaligned, so the selected model is not guaranteed to optimize the reported metric.

Reward weights are treated as hyperparameters tunable on the validation set, but in RL, reward shaping changes the agent's learned objective during training, not just its decision boundary — this conflates objective specification with hyperparameter tuning.

The validation window is only 6 months for an RL model requiring substantial episode rollouts; at 8–12% failure prevalence, this window may not contain enough failure events for reliable FNR estimation.
